content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 16,
1993
Bruce McPherson
305 Beel Drive
Santa Cruz, California
95060
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—93-235
Dear Mr. Mcpherson:
This is in response to your request for advice concerning the
propriety, of using campaign funds under the “personal use”
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
This letter
is based on facts provided in your letter dated June ‘4,
1993,
and
our telephone conversation of June 11,
1993.
QUESTIONS
1.
Under the Act,
may a candidate in a special election
campaign use campaign funds for the following expenditures:
(a)
A $250 expenditure for the candidate to attend a
dinner sponsored by the Dan Lungren for Attorney General
Committee;
(b)
A $100 contribution to the California Republican
Party to help the party conduct a statewide survey on various
issues;
and
(c)
A $100 contribution to the California Republican
Party to assist candidates in the large number of California
special elections this year,
one of which could be yours.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 , .
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
Sections 85800-
85807,
which describe the permissible use of campaign funds,
are
referred toas the “personal use” laws.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A-93-235
Page
2
CONCLUSIONS
1.
A candidate in a special election may make the following
expenditures which are reasonably related to a political purpose
as follows:
(a)
A $250 expenditure to attend a dinner where
Attorney General Dan Lungren is a guest speaker.
(b)
A $100 contribution to the California Republican
Party to help the party conduct a statewide survey on various
issues.
(c)
A $100 contribution to the California Republican
Party to assist candidates in the large number of California
special elections this year,
one of which could be the candidate’s
election, provided the candidate’s funds are segregated from funds
that will be used to make ôontributions to other candidates in any
special election.
ANALYSIS
Any expenditure of campaign funds to seek office must be, at
a minimuiu, reasonably related to a political purpose.
In
addition,
where an expenditure confers a substantial personal
benefit on the candidate or officeholderL the expenditure must be
directly related to a political purpose.
4
(Section 89512.)
You are a candidate for the California State Assembly in a
special election.
You ask whether it is permissible to use
campaign funds to make a $250 contribution to the Dan Lungren for
Attorney General Committee in order for you to attend a dinner
featuring Mr.
Lungren as a guest speaker.
The purpose for
attending the dinner is to learn more about “law and order” issues
in the state and to meet potential supporters in Monterey County
which is part of the Assembly district for which you are a
candidate.
You also ask about two other expenditures:
(1)
a
contribution of $100 to the California Republican Party to help
your party conduct a statewide survey on various issues and
(2)
a
$100 contribution to support the party in its efforts to assist
candidates in the large number of California special elections
this year,
one of which could be yours.
You state that the
efforts of the party could be useful in your own campaign.
2
A “substantial personal benefit” is defined as a direct
personal benefit to the candidate of $100 or more.
(Section
89511(b) (3).)


Our File No. A-93-235
Page
3
These expenditures are reasonably related to a political
purpose, which is to assist you in your candidacy,
and therefore,
they are permissible expenditures of campaign funds.
However,
Section 853 04 continues to prohibit candidates from transferring
campaign funds to any candidate who is sub:ject to any valid
contribution limitations, including special elections and where
there is a valid local contribution limit.
(SEIU v. Fair
Political Practical Commission (9th Cir.
1992)
955 F.2d 1312,
cert. denied,
U.S.
,
112 S.Ct.
3056.)
Section 85304 provides:
No candidate for elective office or committee
controlled by that candidate or candidates for
elective office shall transfer any contribution to
any other candidate for elective office.
Transfers
of funds between candidates or their controlled
committees are prohibited.
Thus, to ensure your second contribution does not result in a
prohibited transfer to another candidate in a special election,
you should advise the party that the contribution should be
segregated from funds that will be used to make contributions to
other candidates in any special election.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
ma/
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division
However, Section 85304 does not limit independent expenditures
made to support or oppose candidates.
(Leidigh Advice Letter,
No.
1—92—547.)
